title: Individual project: Portfolio creation
text:
In this task, you will use your knowledge in object-oriented programming to create an online presence for yourself as a Data Scientist.
There are three aspects to OOP in the wild. First, OOP is a paradigm as you write a program to structure it. Second, OOP is a perspective with which to approach problems and how to see the world. Third, OOP is a mindset with which you can approach problems and act upon the world.
This project tries to introduce you to OOP in the wild. As you create a website with github pages, OOP is not necessary the first mode (web rendering is usually based on static html code that is not a priori OOP. However, to represent complex websites, you use more complex features and using OOP may help you. You will not learn html coding or css style sheets but you will use your knowledge of OOP concepts to instruct github copilot to help you create an interactive, interesting portfolio.
The second aspect is to show your data prowess, and we do this by embedding a shiny application server in your website. Github pages cannot execute code. However, you can use external services like the posit connect cloud to have an interactive window that executes python code. In this window, you are supposed to showcase your previous data science projects. You may investigate yourself how shiny works (and receive a short introduction in the class). Essentially, shiny makes graphs interactive and allows users to interact with your outcome visualisations. As a preparation, please revisit your previous work and find one to three projects, that you would like to highlight. To expand your portfolio, I would recommend you to add new results from other data science projects throughout your studies to showcase what you can do.



To complete this project, you have to create your online presence and fill it with content of your choice. You will not be graded for your actual content or the looks of your website. Instead, you will be graded on how you executed the project and how well you managed to implement OOP principles in the website.
Please investigate my github example here: https://github.com/jugdemon/2026_oop_portfolio
And the corresponding github-pages:
https://jugdemon.github.io/2026_oop_portfolio/
You may either fork the repository (create a copy to start with), or start from scratch. You fill find instructions for both below.

Create your github repository called “portfolio”. This will allow us to create your portfolio/personal website under https://.github.io/portfolio where user is your github user name. a. Use the repo link https://github.com//portfolio.git to setup your cloud computing and visual studio in the following steps
Create a posit connect cloud account via github integration.

Here you can create data spaces for python to host your shiny app and display information interactively.
Select you want to deploy both apps and documents.
Select you prefer python.

Install VS Code and activate all AI features via github integration. If you have the github student version, you get more advanced features. Activate your student version if you haven’t yet. a. Go to the market place and install extensions for python, quarto and shiny.
Start a chat on the right side. Ask the chat to create a project for your portfolio that uses quatro as a website and shiny for the visualization. The shiny server should run on posit cloud using the python version. All the code should be synced to your portfolio repository.

title: Welcome
text:
I am a Data Scientist in training with a passion for turning complex data into interactive stories. This portfolio is a collection of my work, exploring the intersection of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), Data Analysis, and Interactive Web Development.



Interactive Projects: Explore dashboards built with Shiny for Python and Quarto.
OOP Design: See how I use object-oriented principles to build clean, reusable data pipelines.
Data Insights: Deep dives into OECD datasets and more.

Feel free to explore my Projects or learn more About Me.

title: Object-Oriented Data Showcase
text:
This project demonstrates the power of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in data science. Instead of writing procedural scripts, I’ve built a robust data architecture that can simulate, process, and visualize data through a consistent interface.



Base Class: BaseDataProcessor defines the blueprint for all data operations.
Concrete Class: PortfolioData implements specific logic for random walk simulations and statistical distributions.
Extensibility: This pattern allows for easy addition of new data sources and processing methods without breaking existing code.




Below are visualizations generated by the PortfolioData class, demonstrating its ability to manage state and complex transformations.








Figure 1: Random Walk Simulation (500 steps)












Figure 2: Distribution of Simulated Step Increments

title: California Earnings Analysis
text:
This analysis explores the 2014 Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) data from the Current Population Survey (CPS), specifically focused on California. The analysis follows an Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) pattern, utilizing a custom CAEarningsProcessor class to handle data cleaning, transformation, and statistical calculations.



Inheritance: CAEarningsProcessor inherits from BaseDataProcessor, ensuring a consistent interface for data loading and preprocessing.
Encapsulation: Data filtering and grouping logic are encapsulated within class methods, keeping the visualization code clean and focused.




Below are two key insights from the California earnings data:








Figure 1: Mean Weekly Earnings by Education Level and Gender












Figure 2: Gender Wage Gap by Union Membership

title: OECD Health Risk Factors
text:
In this project, I analyze health risk factors across OECD countries using an Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) approach. By structuring the data processing logic into classes, I’ve created a maintainable and scalable analysis pipeline.



OOP Structure: Data loading, cleaning, and filtering are handled by a dedicated OECDDataProcessor class.
Interactive Visualization: Explore trends in alcohol consumption, tobacco use, and obesity across different nations.
Dynamic Filtering: Select specific health measures and countries to see how health trends have evolved over decades.




Below are static visualizations generated from the OECD health risk factors dataset.








Figure 1: Alcohol Consumption Trends in select OECD countries












Figure 2: Top 15 Countries by Obesity Rate (Latest Year)

title: About Me
text:
Who I Am
I am a 3rd-year Data Science and Society student at Central European University, majoring in Data Science and Economics.



Education

BSc in Data Science and Society | Central European University (2023 - 2026)



Technical Skills

Programming: Python (Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Shiny), R, SQL
Software Engineering: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), Git/GitHub
Visualization: Quarto, Tableau, Plotly
Data Science: Machine Learning, Statistical Modeling, Data Pre-processing



Interests

Interactive Visualization: Designing dashboards that make complex data accessible.
Sustainable Data: Exploring environmental datasets through a data-driven lens.
AI Ethics: Understanding the societal impact of algorithmic decision-making.

title: Analysis: The State of Earnings in California (2014)
text:
This report analyzes data from the 2014 Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) of the Current Population Survey (CPS). The focus is on California, examining how weekly earnings are influenced by education, gender, and union membership.
Dataset Overview: - Total California respondents: 12,490 - Overall average weekly earnings: $947.59




Education remains the most significant predictor of weekly earnings.



Earnings by Education Level











Education Level
Male Avg Weekly
Female Avg Weekly
Gender Wage Gap (%)




Less than High School
$533.41
$379.76
28.8%


High School Graduate
$752.16
$568.17
24.5%


Some College
$825.51
$621.88
24.7%


Associate Degree
$1,018.22
$780.15
23.4%


Bachelor’s Degree
$1,418.39
$1,118.62
21.1%


Advanced Degree
$1,903.02
$1,423.33
25.2%





The wage gap persists across all education levels, but is notably different in its magnitude. Interestingly, the gap often widens as education increases, suggesting that while higher education raises the “floor” for everyone, “glass ceilings” may still affect women at higher professional levels.





Union membership has traditionally been seen as a way to standardize wages and reduce inequality.



Gender Wage Gap: Union vs Non-Union











Union Member
Male Avg Weekly
Female Avg Weekly
Gender Wage Gap (%)




No
$1,022.50
$803.35
21.4%


Yes
$1,171.48
$1,015.59
13.3%





Union membership appears to reduce the gender wage gap. For union members, the gap is 13.3%, compared to 21.4% for non-union members.





The data from 2014 California MORG shows clear trends: 1. Education Payoff: There is a clear upward trajectory in earnings associated with education. 2. Persistent Gap: A gender wage gap exists at every educational tier. 3. Union Influence: Unionization significantly impacts wage distribution and the gender gap.

title: Analysis: Hourly Wages by Occupation and Education Level (California)
text:
This report examines the relationship between educational attainment and hourly compensation across the most frequent occupations in California using the 2014 Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) dataset.


The following heatmap displays the average hourly wage for the top 15 occupations by respondent frequency, segmented by their highest level of education. The color scale ranges from grey ($0) to deep green ($50+).



Hourly Wage Heatmap






Educational Premium: In almost every occupation, individuals with a Bachelor’s or Advanced Degree earn significantly more than their counterparts with only a high school education.
High-Wage Leaders: Registered Nurses and Accountants/Auditors with advanced degrees represent the highest earning segments in this dataset, frequently exceeding the $40/hour mark.
The Wage Floor: Entry-level service roles such as Cashiers and Waiters/Waitresses show a much tighter wage distribution, often staying in the grey-to-light-green zone (under $15/hour) regardless of education level.
Specialized vs. General Roles: Professional occupations show a much steeper “return on education” compared to administrative or manual labor roles.




Data derived from the 2014 CPS Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) dataset, filtered specifically for California respondents.
